<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Since we assume T &gt; α 1 + α 2 , we have n ≥ 3. Thus 2 n -3 α 2 = glyph[lscript] n -1 ≤ T and we obtain

2 k -2 glyph[lscript] 2 ≤ 2 n -2 glyph[lscript] 2 = 2 n -2 α 2 ≤ 2 T for 2 ≤ k ≤ n . Therefore, we obtain

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## B Implementation Details

In all problem instances, we consider low-dimensional and nonsparse coefficients. Thus we apply RMLP and RMLP-2 without regularization. We now specify some algorithm inputs.

- Across all the simulation settings and the real-data applications, we use the same value of λ = 0 . 1 , p max = 30 , C = 20 and W = 10 4 . The known bound W of || θ 0 || 1 for DIP and RMLP, and of || θ 0 || 1 + 1 σ for RMLP-2 is mainly for theoretical purpose. Thus we select a random and loose choice W = 10 4 to minimize its affect on the real performance of the three methods.
- In the simulation settings, we use the same value of the first and the second episode lengths α 1 = α 2 = 2 11 . In the real-data applications, the number of data samples is not as many as in the simulation settings and thus we shrink the first and second episode lengths to α 1 = α 2 = 2 10 or 2 9 according to the horizon length.
- For DIP, we use β ∗ t = s ∗ (1 ∨ ( 1 p max √ λd + √ 2 log( T 0 ) + d log( dλ +( t -1) p 2 max dλ )) 2 ) in the UCB construction of Inner Algorithm B with a scaling parameter s ∗ = 1 40 . Such a scaling is common in UCB practices.

- The specified distribution of RMLP is the logistic distribution with CDF e x e x +1 and the specified class of distributions of RMLP-2 is the class of logistic distributions { e ( x -µ ) /s e ( x -µ ) /s +1 | µ ∈ R , s ∈ R + } . We choose logistic distributions since the corresponding optimization problem of RMLP and RMLP-2 can be formulated into modified versions of the conventional logistic regression.

## C Detailed Discussion of Instability of RMLP-2

We provide a more detailed discussion of why large estimation errors sometimes happen with RMLP-2 as indicated by the simulation Examples 7 - 8. Note that RMLP-2 uses a mapping function to map the customer covariates to the set prices. Therefore, the set prices together with the customer covariates lie in a lower-dimensional subspace. It turns out that such subspace is likely to lie almost on only one side of the Bayes decision boundary when the distribution of x glyph[latticetop] t θ 0 is positively away from 0 by the nature of the mapping function. This is exactly the case for Examples 7 - 8. Then with such singular data structure and the wrongly specified distribution class, the RMLP-2 estimation process, which borrows a maximum likelihood idea, becomes unstable especially for higher dimensions. On the other hand, the distribution of x glyph[latticetop] t θ 0 is not positively away from 0 in Example 9, thus yielding relatively stable estimations.

## D Detailed Estimation Procedure of θ 0 and F for Real AutoLoan Data

We take the dataset of the entire US as an example to illustrate how to estimate the noise CDF F and θ 0 . The estimation of state-specific F and θ 0 are similar. Let T 0 = 208085 be the number of data points for the US.

The model we consider can be simply written as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Note that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ˜ F ( · ) = F ( · + F -1 ( 1 2 )) and ˜ x t = (1 , x glyph[latticetop] t ) glyph[latticetop] , ˜ θ 0 = ( F -1 ( 1 2 ) , θ glyph[latticetop] 0 ) glyph[latticetop] . Then ˜ F -1 ( -1 2 ) = 0. Thus without loss of generality, we can assume F -1 ( 1 2 ) = 0 by adding an all-1 column to x t and an intercept term to θ 0 . Then we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Therefore, we can form a classification problem with responses { y t } t ∈ [ T 0 ] and covariates { ( x glyph[latticetop] t , p t ) glyph[latticetop] } t ∈ [ T 0 ] . It admits the Bayes decision boundary { u : ( θ glyph[latticetop] 0 , -1) u = 0 } involving θ 0 . We use logistic regression to obtain an estimate ( ˆ β glyph[latticetop] , ˆ b ) of a multiple of ( θ glyph[latticetop] 0 , -1). Then we use ˆ θ 0 = -ˆ β b as the estimate of θ 0 .

After obtaining the estimate ˆ θ 0 of θ 0 , we step forward to estimate F . Denote the corresponding PDF function as f . Our final aim would be generating an appropriate estimate of f as a weighted average of normal distribution PDFs. Then we are able to construct a suitable closed form of an estimate ˆ F of F as a weighted average of normal distribution CDFs. The data closely related to the behavior of F can be formulated as { ( p t -x glyph[latticetop] t θ 0 , y t ) } 1 ≤ t ≤ T 0 . However, we do not know the true values of p t -x glyph[latticetop] t θ 0 since θ 0 is unknown. By substituting ˆ θ 0 for θ 0 , we obtain the approximate dataset { ( p t -x glyph[latticetop] t ˆ θ 0 , y t ) } 1 ≤ t ≤ T 0 . Since y t ∼ Ber(1 -F ( p t -x glyph[latticetop] t θ 0 )), we have for any v ∈ R ,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where w is some pre-specified window size parameter. Thus by this approximation strategy

we are able to estimate the value of F at the point v . Then we use the difference quotient ˜ F w ( v + w ) -F w ( v -w ) 2 w as an estimate ˜ f w ( v ) of f at the point v . We find that most of the data in { ( p t -x glyph[latticetop] t ˆ θ 0 , y t ) } 1 ≤ t ≤ T 0 belong to the range [ -5 , 30]. Thus we formulate a discrete set V = { v 1 , . . . , v 7 } on this range where v i = -7 . 5 + 5 i . Here we use a moderate window size w = 2 with respect to the dataset size and the length of the objective range to obtain the satisfactory estimates Est( V ) = { ˜ f w ( v 1 ) , . . . , ˜ f w ( v 7 )) } of f on V . The left image in Figure 13 shows the discrete estimates Est( V ). Based on these discrete estimates, we are able to formulate a closed form estimate of f on the entire real line by an approach similar in idea with the kernel density estimation. Namely, we can construct the estimate ˆ f raw = ∑ 7 i =1 ˜ f w ( v i ) ∑ 7 j =1 ˜ f w ( v j ) φ ( v i , σ 2 ) where φ ( v i , σ 2 ) is the PDF function of N ( v i , σ 2 ) distribution. Here we use a choice of σ = 3 to obtain an estimate ˆ f raw with appropriate smoothness.

Figure 13: Estimation procedure of the noise PDF for the US data.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Note that the true F satisfies F -1 (0) = 1 2 and thus has the same mass on both sides of 0. However, by our choice of V ⊆ [ -5 , 30] and the kernel smoothing approach, ˆ f raw places much more mass on the positive side of 0. The simplest way to remedy this problem is to involve more discrete estimates of the pdf f on the negative side of 0. This helps make the sum of discrete estimates ˜ f w ( v ) on both sides of 0 equal. Then by the nature of our kernel smoothing approach, the resulting smoothed estimate ˆ f yields an ˆ F approximately satisfying ˆ F -1 ( 1 2 ) = 0. However, using the same way to estimate f ( v ) for v / ∈ [ -5 , 30] is not reliable because of high variance and instability caused by lack of data outside the range [ -5 , 30]. Thus we simply expand V, Est( V ) into V ∗ , Est( V ∗ ) in a smooth way. Specifically, let V ∗ = { v -8 , . . . , v 0 , v 1 , . . . , v 9 } where v i = -7 . 5 + 5 i . Then ˜ f w ( v -8 ) , . . . , ˜ f w ( v 0 ) are well

chosen for smoothness and to satisfy ∑ v i &lt; 0 ˜ f w ( v i ) = ∑ v i &gt; 0 ˜ f w ( v i ). The expanded discrete estimates Est( V ∗ ) are shown in the middle image in Figure 13. Our final estimates of f and F admit the forms

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where Φ( v i , σ 2 ) is the CDF function of N ( v i , σ 2 ) distribution. The right image in Figure 13 shows the final smoothed estimate of the noise PDF.

In Figure 14, we present the similar estimation procedure of the noise PDF for the California data.

Figure 14: Estimation procedure of the noise PDF for the California data.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## E DIP-SVM: A Variant of DIP

We propose a variant DIP-SVM of our DIP algorithm in the following Algorithm 6. Instead of applying logistic regression, it uses Support Vector Machine (SVM) as the linear classifier in Inner Algorithm A to estimate θ 0 . For the simulation setting in Example 1, we conduct the regret comparison between DIP and DIP-SVM, along with RMLP and RMLP-2. Figure 15 shows that DIP-SVM performs similar to DIP, and both outperform RMLP-2 and RMLP. This suggests that the performance of DIP policy is relatively robust to the choice of the linear classifier. In this paper, we suggest to use logistic regression in DIP policy due to its stable performance and cheap computation.

## Algorithm 6 DIP-SVM

- 1: Input: (at time 0) α 1 , α 2 , p max , C, λ, W
- 2: Input: (arrives over time) covariates { x t } t ∈ [ T ]
- 3: For time t = 1 , . . . , glyph[lscript] 1 (= α 1 ), do
- 4: Set a price p t randomly from (0 , p max ) and receive a binary response y t .
- 5: For episodes k = 2 , 3 , . . . , n (= n ( T, α 1 , α 2 )), do
- 6: Apply the SVM version of Inner Algorithm A with the input data { ( x t , p t , y t ) } ∑ k -2 i =1 glyph[lscript] i +1 ≤ t ≤ ∑ k -1 i =1 glyph[lscript] i and W to obtain the estimate ˆ θ k -1 .
- 7: Apply Inner Algorithm B on the coming sequential covariates { x t } ∑ k -1 i =1 glyph[lscript] i +1 ≤ t ≤ ∑ k i =1 glyph[lscript] i , with the estimate ˆ θ k -1 , discretization number d k = C glyph[ceilingleft] (2 k -2 glyph[lscript] 2 ) 1 6 glyph[ceilingright] and the UCB construction in (2) with β t = β ∗ t and δ = 1 2 k -2 glyph[lscript] 2 .

Figure 15: Regret comparison with DIP-SVM.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## F Real data Analysis in Four More US States

We provide additional real data analysis on the Texas, Florida, Pennsylvania and Virginia data. As shown in Figure 16, DIP shows a clear sublinear trend while RMLP and RMLP-2 do not. Specifically, DIP outperforms both RMLP and RMLP-2 for the Texas and Pennsylvania state data just like that in the original cases of the US and California state data. For the Florida and Virginia state data, RMLP-2 performs better than DIP within the limited time horizon. However, considering the increasing trend of RMLP-2 and DIP, we expect that DIP would eventually outperform RMLP-2 as the time horizon gets longer.
